data.title: Synopsys: Load Up While The Market Ignores
data.description:
Synopsys: A Gift Or Not?

A gift or not a gift? Investors who saw the stunning decline in the stock of Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS) are arguably trying to assess whether it is picking up the falling knife. Following an initial post-earnings crash that saw SNPS give up more than 30%, dip buyers appeared ready to defend it against a further slide, as SNPS regained almost 13% to finish Thursday's trading.

As a software company focusing on the foundry customers within the semiconductor value chain, investing in SNPS was always going to be mired in concentration risks, isn't it? Think about it. How many leading-edge foundries do we have in the market right now? I'm sure you can easily name three: TSMC (TSM), Intel (INTC), and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF).

Hence, a wrong bet could also have substantive ramifications on the backlog and growth prospects. In the case of Synopsys, while management didn't specify the exact customer's name, it's not rocket science for us to make an educated guess that Intel's fumbles with its 18A process roadmap has had a significant impact on Synopsys's IP business. And if we add on the geopolitical headwinds emanating from SNPS's Chinese exposure, it seems like a double whammy of sorts affecting it all at the same time. Even if it doesn't quite qualify as a perfect storm, calling it a maelstrom wouldn't have surprised investors.

Synopsys: Don't Be Mistaken About Its Core Drivers

Yet, I don't think any SNPS investor is mistaken about the core growth drivers underlining its dominance and competitive moat. It's clear that EDA will remain as its most significant growth vector through the rest of the decade. Management promulgated that the tailwinds from increasingly complex high-performance computing and AI workloads will be key underpinnings for SNPS's design automation business.

And when the company manages to fully integrate the synergies and capabilities originating from Ansys, it should strengthen its offerings, while driving demand for Synopsys' emulation and prototyping solutions. In addition, SNPS has also been focusing on using AI-powered EDA, and has secured 20 customers in the pilot phase, opening up another potentially beneficial growth pillar to its design business.

Also, I think the market might have also underplayed possibilities from the transition into more customized subsystems, which will most certainly increase the deal size and royalties opportunities that should benefit its IP business, that is currently under siege.

SNPS: EDA Remains The Key Pillar

Hence, I'm perplexed why the market decided to eviscerate the YTD gains accrued painstakingly by SNPS investors due to near-term weaknesses imputed to its IP business. Notably, the IP business suffered an 8% decline due to the challenges highlighted earlier, even though the EDA business notched a 23% YoY rise, including revenue from Ansys. And with EDA still accounting for more than 70% of the company's overall trailing twelve months revenue, I believe the negative reaction from the market appears to be too dramatic than what the numbers suggest.

Nevertheless, I can comprehend the disappointment, as execution was clearly amiss with its FQ3 miss, coupled with lower-than-expected guidance, further hampering sentiments. While I think the market has the right to air its grievances (making it loud and clear), it seems to have openly ignored the long-term confidence from management in its ability to recover its IP business, while also riding on the structural tailwinds that should bolster its EDA business.

As a reminder, management hasn't wavered from its commitment to deliver a mid-40% operating margin outlook, which I thought should have allayed fears of a structural decline. Wall Street's estimates suggest an adjusted operating margin of 39.6% for FY2026, indicating that while near-term profitability headwinds in FY2025 could hamper its business, we shouldn't expect integration risks to materially affect its margin trajectory.

Buy Or Sell SNPS Stock?

And I guess the market seems to agree with that proposition based on the almost 13% recovery yesterday. If you glean the chart above, we can clearly observe robust buying support that has benefited SNPS over the past two years whenever the stock falls toward its 10Y average of 21.7x. At that level, buyers had consistently returned with a fervor to defend it against a further slide.

And with the stock still hovering close to that level due to its recent plunge, I believe investors should seriously consider capitalizing on a solid opportunity to buy into a high-quality software play with a highly robust and profitable business model.

While I'm not about to ignore the China-related risks that could still inflict unanticipated damage on buying sentiments, or the uncertainties relating to Intel's business, I believe these headwinds seem to have been de-risked sufficiently for us to take a more bullish posture now.

Rating: Buy.

data.title: Constellation Software: An AI Winner In The Shadows
data.description:
Constellation Software (TSX:CSU:CA) is one of the most special companies in the world. I would say it is the best serial acquirer in existence. It is commanded by one of the best and strangest CEOs on the planet, who has 6 executing arms with the power to make decentralized M&A decisions.

There are two narratives that have been prevailing in the market for some time now:

  • Constellation has run out of acquisition TAMs in the Vertical Software (VMS) segment.
  • AI will be disruptive to their business.

In this article my aim is to question these two points.

Moreover, my current interest in the company arises because it is a company that I have known well for years (I have been holding it since 2021 at $1600 in a portfolio I have with my father, but not in my personal one) and because it has never fallen more than 30% in its history. Well, recently, it dropped almost -20% from all-time highs.

All values are in CAD despite the $ symbol.

Inorganic Growth

It all starts at Constellations's annual meeting in May of this year.

It is a fact that CSU is reaching a considerable size.

Despite having a TAM of acquisitions in the Vertical Software niches in which the company is fully specialized (40,000-80,000 possible companies), many are small in size, which does not “move the needle” when they buy them, and they have to buy an increasing number of companies, as is shown in the second row.

What is starting to be raised in the company is:

  • Either larger purchases with lower returns (closer to 15%-20% vs. the current 25%-30%).
  • Expansion into other sectors that are not related to the company's traditional expertise but still offer returns of +25%.

And at the annual meeting, it was strongly implied that they are seriously considering this. They say they have full confidence in CSU's decentralized structure and will rely on what the heads of the divisions will buy.

Constellation Software operates as a decentralized holding company where its 6 parent companies (Vertical Market Software groups) operate autonomously, buying and managing niche software companies with minimal central intervention. These 6 groups are: Volaris Group, Harris Computer Systems, Jonas Software, Perseus Group, Trapeze Group, and Vela Software.

So it is quite possible that over time we will see CSU enter new market niches, which we probably can't even think of right now.

An example of this could be payments, as they said at their annual meeting:

We're definitely focused on payments, and we've made a couple of acquisitions in that space.

We're looking at others, and I think it's something that can bring tremendous value.

And what we see from our competitors in this space is a lot of payments and software coupling.

So if we want to remain competitive and leaders in our field, we have to be better on the payments side.

So we'll continue to make inroads in that space and, hopefully, we'll get better quarter over quarter.

- Barry Symons, CEO, Annual General Meeting 2025

Organic Growth

CSU's organic growth has always been an issue, hence the spin-offs of Topicus and Lumin, which did have more organic growth and could benefit from their expertise to drive this part of the growth equation.

Anyway, something seems to have changed for the better in this industry after the rise of AI.

They commented that AI can help vertical software companies a lot because it will save a lot of time and effort when it comes to chopping code, making it economically viable, being able to add additional services to those VMS at a reduced price, and unlocking pricing power never unlocked before. This could solve one of the biggest fears that has always surrounded the matrix.

This started to show in the Q2 results.

Constellation has achieved its highest organic growth since 2023. The stock was up 4.4% on the day of the presentation, which, for a company like CSU, is a significant move.

Otherwise:

Revenue: +15% to $2.84B [Organic growth: +5%]

-Net revenue: -68% to $56M

-CFO: +63% to $433M

-FCF to shareholders: +20% to $220M

-Acquisitions: $380M aggregate cash consideration ($469M incl. deferred payments)

Very important growth in Maintenance & Other Recurring (+7% organic, the fastest in the group), which, as its name indicates, is the most recurring part of the company.

Right now the organics at Constellation are quite important, or more than they were before, because ex-organic M&A would be 10%, which is fine, but far from what it was.... In fact, they are not managing to deploy 100% of what they generate, although it is also true that they have not made any significant large acquisitions.

One month after these results, my feelings that the AI will eventually benefit Constellation are becoming more and more latent every day. It will be possible to obtain double-digit EBIT growth in the mid-single digits, with low organic sales growth (e.g., 2%).

The folks at In Practise published a piece of an interview with a Topicus executive, in which they commented as follows:

If you look into the main cost, it's just DevOps. Seventy percent of the cost I had at Topicus was DevOps because everything we are doing is extensive. For example, if I had a proposition with revenue of about €10 million, I had 62 DevOps working on it, which means about €6.2 million of my costs were just developers. With AI advancements in fiber coding and prompt engineering, and the speed and complexity you can achieve in days instead of weeks, I can't see another way than Topicus postponing a lot of their DevOps in a few years. The revenue stream will remain the same or increase because you can create more solutions, but the costs of these solutions will decline over the years. I think it will decline extensively. The 60 people you need now might be reduced to 20 or 25 people in three years to do the same work

Future IRRs from Acquisitions

As the weeks went by, there continued to be a lot of talk about the rates of return that acquisitions (larger or from different fields) could yield from now on.

For this, it is best to get an answer from senior management at the annual meeting.

Mark Leonard. Founder, President, and Chairman

Historically, we haven't done a great job predicting how long the road ahead is, so use that as context. And what you say is probably not based on a lot of forecasting experience.

When we look at the market, I think we do a reasonably good job of covering and knowing what acquisitions are happening in our space. And the number of acquisitions that we're successful in I'm not uncomfortable with. Now, that doesn't mean we're always the preferred buyer. We lose a lot more than we gain. And we end up having to follow literally tens of thousands of acquisition prospects. Every year we add to that base with new prospects that we follow.

But the quality of the additions to our funnel is not nearly as good as it used to be, say, 10 years ago. Ten years ago, it would have been well-defined vertical market software. It would have been in geographies we knew and loved. Today, it tends to be in much more distant geographies and it's less strictly defined.

Now we look at horizontals. We look at hybrid software and hardware companies. We look at hybrid software and data companies. You'll see that we're experiencing what investors call a style drift. And that's often associated with less good performance.

So far, we have not seen that weaker performance. We continue to achieve the minimum rates of return we set, while trying to deploy our capital.

This other commentary on how AI will affect software also strikes me as valuable:

Barry Symons. Chief Executive Officer (CEO)

It may come down a little bit, but the barrier to entry will always be there. As long as we do our job - and the key is going to be the people we have running these businesses, doing their job and making sure they stay ahead of the competition - we'll be fine.

The other trick for us will be that, while I'd love to say that we're perfect and we never make mistakes, the truth is that we do. And there will be some businesses that miss the boat, and a competitor will come along, catch up and may even overtake them.

At that point, our job will be to decide whether it's a great allocation of capital to keep chasing that market or whether the better option is to not chase it as aggressively, but to focus on strong retention and use that capital on another business that hasn't missed the train and accelerate it.

So we're thinking about all of those things, and I get a little bit obsessed with it as well. But I think, if we act rationally and have great people working for us, we will solve most of the problems. Still, we will definitely make mistakes, there's no doubt about that.

Capital Allocation

The main metric Constellation uses to measure the performance of its business is free cash flow available to shareholders (FCFA2S). And it is calculated as follows.

Constellation is a curious company. It has never repurchased shares and always pays out $85M in dividends on a fixed basis. The way it returns capital to shareholders is in the form of FCFA2S growth through capital deployed in acquisitions. The table shows Constellation's OCF, FCFA2S, and cash spent on acquisitions. It is positive that they are able to deploy 100% of their capital in M&A, because it means they still find investment opportunities.

Valuation

Since Constellation is a company that does not return capital directly to its shareholders, I believe the best way to value it is through an inverse DCF with a 10% minimum discount rate and a 3% TGR.

Constellation has generated $1.9 billion in FCFA2S (adjusted for the IRGA obligation) in the last 12 months. At a price of CAD4420 per share, the market is discounting 19.5% growth in this figure over the next 10 years. These figures are close to what it has done historically, but I need a little more margin of safety to be able to add it to the portfolio.

Future growth in this figure will depend on how much capital they can deploy in the future and the ROIC of these acquisitions, although we will also have to add future organic growth, which, as I have already shown, may increase in the coming years.

That is why I rate the stock as a hold.

Risks

The first risk that Constellation is often blamed for lately is that AI greatly lowers the cost of programming and therefore the barriers to entry. This could be a problem for many companies, but I believe that the dynamics of the VMS are somewhat different.

VMS is software that represents such a small percentage of a company's expenses (2%-5% of OPEX) and is so critical that I don't think there is much interest in programming one's own, with all the execution risks that this may entail.

Moreover, if our thesis holds true, Constellation will be able to add many extra functionalities to this software thanks precisely to AI, making the product more sticky and raising prices by capturing a percentage of this extra value and increasing the organic growth that historically has not been so high.

On the other hand, the second big risk, and one in which you have to trust the management team and the good work of Constellation's subsidiaries, is the integration of possible new acquisitions that may be either larger or outside Constellation's expertise. We will only know this with the passage of time, but I am confident in their good work.

Conclusions

Looking at all this, I think it is clear that AI, far from hurting a company like CSU, will benefit it with its organic growth.

The problem where we lose visibility is inorganic growth. It is not a problem of TAM of vertical software companies; as we have already seen, it is a problem that CSU is getting bigger and bigger, and it is increasingly difficult to move the figures of a giant.

While the last 2-3 years have had record numbers of capital deployments, it doesn't sound to me like there used to be such clear talk about venturing into new industries outside of VMS.

In this matter there is no choice but to trust Mark Leonard and the rest of the team, who have proven for decades to be a company completely outside of conventional market norms.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Carlsberg: Shares Still Look Cheap Amid A Challenging Consumer Backdrop
data.description:
2025 is shaping up to be an odd year for Danish brewer Carlsberg (OTCPK:CABGY) (OTCPK:CABJF). While weak consumer sentiment continues to weigh on its business, that hasn't prevented the stock from being a strong performer. Indeed, Carlsberg's ADSs have returned over 30% since my last update back in January - good for around 20 points of outperformance compared to the global staples space and the S&P 500. True, currency movements do explain much of this. The dollar has fallen by double digits against the Danish krone in that time. Still, the native performance has also been solid, with the Copenhagen-listed shares delivering over 13% price appreciation.

With H1 2025 results to digest, now is a good time to revisit the investment case here. I remain bullish, with Carlsberg still looking cheap relative to historical marks.

Operating Environment Remains Challenging

Although this isn't a company-specific problem, weak consumer sentiment has nonetheless continued to be a headwind for Carlsberg this year. On the surface, the firm's reported numbers look strong. Total beverage volumes came in at 76.3 million hectoliters over the first six months of 2025, up around 16% year-on-year. Revenue of DKK45.9 billion (~$7.2 billion) was around 18% higher, while operating profit jumped 15% to around DKK7.2 billion (~$1.1 billion).

These figures are flattered by the firm's acquisition of U.K. soft drinks manufacturer Britvic, which closed back in January. As a result, reported non-beer volumes rose over 90% year-on-year to 23.6 million hectoliters.

Trends look much softer on an organic basis, with volumes down 1.7% year-on-year. The expiration of the San Miguel license in the U.K. contributed around 130 basis points to this figure. It would be reasonable to look past this, in which case organic volumes were down around 0.4% year-on-year. Revenue growth was more resilient thanks to price hikes and a positive mix effect (more on that below), rising 1.3% on an organic basis (again excluding the San Miguel impact), though this is still below the firm's longer-term ambition (mid-single-digit growth).

Again, it is important to point out that this isn't a company-specific issue. Peers are also seeing soft volume growth right now. For example, Heineken's (OTCQX:HEINY) consolidated volumes slipped 0.7% in the first half of the year. They fell 2% at Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD). The simple fact is that consumer sentiment is currently at a cyclical soft point.

This appears to be particularly true in Asia, which is geared to Chinese economic development and accounts for ~40% of Carlsberg's total beer volumes. It is also the company's most profitable geographic segment. In the first half, Carlsberg's Asian beer volumes declined by 1.7%, well below the circa 4% growth rate the company averaged before COVID.

Bright Spots Under The Hood

While soft consumer sentiment is weighing on volumes, there are some bright spots in the business. For example, premium and non-alcoholic beer trends remain relatively more robust. These are key long-term growth categories for both Carlsberg and the wider industry, offsetting the more structurally challenged outlook for mainstream/economy brands. Premium volumes rose 5% in H1, including 16% growth for premium flagship brand Carlsberg. Alcohol-free beer volumes rose 7%.

A structurally better growth profile is not the only attractive thing about premium brands. They also tend to be higher-margin products, and this can drive a positive mix effect. This was on display in H1. While organic volume growth was negative, gross profit per hectoliter rose 3%. This drove 1% organic growth in overall gross profit, while EBITDA and EBIT both rose around 2.5% on the same basis.

At 46%, Carlsberg's gross margin remains around 350-400 basis points below its pre-COVID level—which is a legacy of the post-pandemic bout of inflation. Management aims to restore this ratio in the coming years, which would boost the firm's earnings growth. Growing the premium portfolio ahead of overall portfolio growth will be an important tool in achieving this.

Sequential trends also offer some encouragement. Although H1 volumes were weak overall, this was skewed toward Q1. Organic volume growth was actually modestly positive in Q2, clocking in at 0.2%. Ditto with organic revenue growth, which was ~110 basis points higher in Q2 (+2.4%) compared to the overall H1 print (+1.3%). Looking ahead, management does remain cautious regarding the consumer environment, but full-year organic EBIT growth was still tightened upwards to 3%-5% (from 1%-5%), which would be an improvement on the 2.3% growth recorded in H1.

Carlsberg's Valuation

Through the cycle, Carlsberg targets 4%-6% annual organic sales growth and, given cost leverage, a slightly higher rate of organic operating profit. While it is currently below this trend, I would argue that this is still reflected in its valuation. At DKK776.80 as I type (around $24.40 per ADS), Carlsberg trades for around 13.3x consensus 2025 EPS. That looks attractive compared to its past average, with the stock historically trading for closer to 20x EPS based on its year-end level.

Granted, we do need to make some allowances for the company's elevated debt levels. Due to the Britvic deal, Carlsberg ended H1 with roughly DKK65 billion in net interest-bearing debt on its balance sheet, equal to around 3.4x 12-month pro-forma EBITDA. That is well above its long-run average.

Even so, debt-neutral valuation measures also point to undervaluation. Given DKK13.9 billion in consensus 2025 operating profit, DKK65 billion in net debt, and a DKK108 billion market cap, Carlsberg's EV/EBIT multiple is currently around 12.4x. This is also below its recent historical average.

Carlsberg aims to reduce net debt towards a 2x EBITDA level. It is generating plenty of surplus cash to do so. Analysts expect around DKK24 billion in total 2025-2027 free cash flow, easily enough to cover both the cost of its dividend (currently around DKK3.75 billion per annum) and pay down debt.

Also, it's not like analysts are bearish regarding the company's medium-term growth prospects. While recent comps have been a bit soft, volumes, revenue, margins, and, ultimately, earnings are still expected to grow nicely over the next 2-3 years. As a result, analysts see EPS reaching around DKK70 by 2027 (~$2.20 per ADS). Even on a flat multiple, that would put the stock at around DKK930 (~$29.20 per ADS) in a couple of years. With dividends, today's buyer would be looking at a comfortable double-digit annualized return. For that reason, I remain bullish, and I am leaving my "buy" rating for Carlsberg in place.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Short Selling Indicators Point To Higher Prices
data.description:
(This is the second in a series of educational articles on investor sentiment and the different ways to measure it. The first article was: The option market says prices are going higher.

This second article explains how to use buying in ProShare short funds to measure investor expectations for higher or lower prices).

Short Selling Indicators

During the heyday of technical analysis from 1960 to 1985, the best indicators to signal bear market lows were the short selling ratios. After correctly signaling the bear market lows of 1962, 1966, 1970, and 1974, with no false signals in between, short-selling indicators became the primary technical tool when looking for the end of a bear market.

Low levels of short selling were also good at locating market tops, as Marty Zweig showed in his famous Barron’s article, “The Dearth of Short Selling,” published in March of 1972, right before the two-year bear market.

Unfortunately, with the emergence of index futures and arbitrage short selling in the mid-1980s, it became impossible to distinguish arbitrage short selling from normal short selling, and short selling indicators faded away.

The ProShare Short Selling Ratio

This changed with the creation of ProShare short funds in 2006.

The ProShare fund family has 140 ETFs that invest in exotic ways, which includes long and short funds that use leverage. To develop good short-selling indicators, we found investor activity in the 2X ultra funds to be the best.

By using sales and open interest numbers, we calculate buying levels in both long and short 2X funds over 20 days. Twenty days provides enough data to assess long-term perspectives. By comparing buying in “short” funds versus “long” funds, we gain insight into what investors expect the market to do.

While some of these ProShare funds were started in 2006, it took until 2010 for the situation to stabilize with enough assets in both types of funds to produce meaningful ratios.

Numbers show that traders buy more “short” funds at market lows and fewer “short” funds at market highs, similar to what they did with actual short selling in the 1960s and 70s. This chart shows that when 1.6 times or more money goes into 2X short funds than 2X long funds, it's a good buying opportunity.

The opposite is true with low ratios, which represent low levels of short selling. A low ratio is .4 or less.

Low ratios are not as good at timing the top as high ratios are at the bottom. One reason for this is that market tops are more obscure and harder to define.

The current ratio is .8. There has never been a significant market decline in the last 15 years that started with a ratio this high.

In other words, like the two option ratios in the first article, the ProShare "short" to "long" buying ratio suggests that prices are not ready to decline.

The Sentiment King Red Zone-Green Zone Ranking

This next chart graphs the ProShare “short” versus “long” buying ratio on the Sentiment King Green Zone Red zone ranking system, which is explained here: The Red Zone Green Zone Ranking System. This way of measuring an indicator allows us to compare all sentiment indicators on the same ranking scale.

Using this system, an investor only needs to worry if an indicator enters either the green or red zone. Each number represents the same historical position of that indicator against past history. This is a major breakthrough, as it allows us to evaluate and combine different types of sentiment indicators on the same scale.

You can see that the current position of this indicator is nowhere near a Red Zone reading that normally occurs before a major top.

The Master Sentiment Indicator

No one indicator is the “best.” Some sentiment indicators work better in some types of markets than others; that's why combining nine indicators into one produces a much more reliable sentiment indicator. The red zone/green zone ranking system allows this to happen for the first time.

The ProShare short selling indicator is one of nine indicators that go into making up our Master Sentiment Indicator. This table shows the 9 indicators that make up the MSI at the top. It shows where each ranks on the universal ranking scale. We've circled the position of the ProShare short indicator. The Master Sentiment Indicator is on the far right.

We have two master sentiment indicators. One for the long term and one for the intermediate term. This table is a week old. We update these on a daily and weekly basis for our subscribers.

data.title: Is The Children's Place Turning A New Leaf?
data.description:
The Children's Place (NASDAQ:PLCE) has experienced rollercoaster price action since I last covered the apparel retailer back in March. During the spring, PLCE stock held up relatively well, as perhaps some of the positive aspects of its Q4 earnings release helped to counter macro concerns like tariffs, but by June, when the company released Q1 FY2025 results, bearishness renewed, leading to further declines for this would-be turnaround play.

More recently, however, bullishness has renewed for PLCE stock. Earlier this month, the company released its quarterly results. The earnings release elicited a bullish reaction by the market, but you may not necessarily want to jump to the conclusion that The Children's Place is turning a new leaf.

With much of the post-earnings rally perhaps fueled by factors besides fundamentals, it's unclear whether this is the start of a recovery, or merely a temporary "dead cat bounce" for PLCE.

Although there are still numerous signs that, in time, this stock could be a profitable name to buy while it remains at bargain basement prices, caution may remain the key word here.

The Children's Place: Recap and Recent Developments

My last article on this stock laid out a cautiously-bullish take on PLCE. In a nutshell, at first I laid out the recent backstory with The Children's Place, how Mithaq Capital has gained control of the company, and the pros and cons of making a long-term wager on this high-risk, high-reward turnaround play.

I also touched on the stock's high short interest, and how that could impact volatility, especially around earnings releases. As mentioned above, the market at first held steady in its sentiment about PLCE stock, but by the summer, bearishness intensified once again, as weak Q1 results, including a worse-than-expected drop in overall sales, and a double-digit drop in e-commerce sales, cast doubt that the situation here was continuing to improve.

In the weeks following the Q1 earnings release, PLCE hit new 52-week lows, and the story here appeared to be shifting from bad to worse. Even as the stock briefly experienced a temporary rally during July, once again this appeared to be short-term noise, the prelude to another round of major price declines.

That said, following the release of Q2 results, sentiment has turned on a dime. However, was it for the right reasons? Let's take a closer look at the results, updates to guidance, and other factors to determine the answer to this question.

PLCE Stock Q2 2025 Earnings

Post-market on June 5, 2025, The Children's Place released results for the company's fiscal quarter ending Aug. 2, 2025. For the quarter, Children's Place reported a 6.8% YoY decline in net sales, down $21.7 million from $319.7 million to $298 million. During the quarter, both brick-and-mortar and e-commerce sales were lower on a YoY basis, but net revenue did come in ahead of sell-side forecasts.

Gross margins also declined YoY, by 100 basis points to 34%. However, thanks to lower SG&A costs, given as last year, the company had heavy one-time restructuring charges, PLCE reported positive operating income for the quarter, coming in at $4.1 million, versus a loss of $21.8 million during the prior year's quarter. Still, as much of the improvement was due to the absence of one-time charges, there was no material improvement in PLCE's adjusted bottom line during the quarter.

For the recent quarter, adjusted net loss came in at $3.4 million, or 15 cents per share. Compare that to a year ago, when The Children's Place reported adjusted net income of $3.9 million, or 30 cents per share. Furthermore, adjusted net losses came in wider than expected by analysts.

Having said all of this, while the results themselves could be regarded as being mixed in nature, why did the market react so positively to these numbers? For one, it's likely that rather than focusing on the murky picture presented by the raw numbers, investors bought into the upbeat narrative provided by CEO Muhammad Umair in the earnings press release.

Per prepared statements by Umair, " July marked the first month in the last 18 months in which the Company’s owned and operated direct-to-consumer business generated positive comparative sales growth," a statement that the market has perhaps been viewed as a sign of further operational improvements ahead.

In other statements, Umair indicated that The Children's Place has reduced outstanding inventory by $78 million over the past twelve months, and plans to implement a new series of cost-savings measures could result in "$40 million of gross benefits over the next three years."

Umair also touched on the subject of tariffs, noting that while the there is going to be a $20 million-$25 million increase in tariff expenses this fiscal year, 80% of this increased expense is set to be mitigated by "a range of strategic initiatives" like diversified sourcing and lower shipping costs.

Alongside management's positive spin on the latest results, the fact that PLCE is still being targeted as a possible "short squeeze stock" may also explain why shares have performed so well in the aftermath of earnings. According to Seeking Alpha, PLCE's short interest currently stands at around 14.6%.

Not only that, with Mithaq Capital and other insiders holding nearly 70% of PLCE's outstanding shares, short interest represents a disproportionate share of the outstanding float. While this can mean big volatility to the downside when the company reports even just positively-negative news, in situations where PLCE has some positive news to tell, it can have an outsized impact on share price performance.

PLCE Stock Valuation

Currently, The Children's Place has a market cap of around $162.5 million. Add in $566.1 million in debt and lease liabilities, and subtract $7.8 million in cash, and total enterprise value comes to around $720.8 million.

Over the trailing twelve month (TTM) period, PLCE has reported EBITDA of around $51.9 million, giving the stock a TTM EV/EBITDA ratio of around 13.9x. This valuation is in line with that of other apparel retail stocks, many of which currently trade at TTM EBITDA multiples in the 10x-15x range.

That said, it should be noted that it may be questionable whether the coming two quarters, ending November 2025 and February 2026, much like this recent quarter, will contain YoY decreases in net revenue, EBITDA, operating income, and net income.

For instance, during the prior year's quarter, the company reported EBITDA of $21.8 million, versus just $12.1 million during the just-completed quarter. The quarters ending November 2024 and February 2025 had reported EBITDA of $34.9 million and $28.8 million, respectively.

PLCE's management may be focused on making long-term improvements for the future, but in the meantime, profitability (as measured by EBITDA) could worsen before it makes a major improvement for the better.

Back in April, when I had just the results through November 2024 to work with, it appears that a Children's Place turnaround could eventually result in the company reporting EBITDA of nearly $100 million. Even after major shareholder dilution, attaining such operational improvement would likely have a major positive impact on the stock.

Now, however, I am skeptical whether EBITDA for the current fiscal year ending February 1, 2026 will stay at levels comparable to that of the trailing twelve month period, especially given the $15.8 million EBITDA loss reported during Q1 FY2025.

Sure, further challenges in the near-term could give way to dramatically better results in the long-run. Then again, maybe not. From Umair's commentary, it's unclear whether the latest restructuring plans will result in annual cost savings of $40 million within three years, or if this estimate represents the aggregation of potential cost savings across each of the next three years.

Rather than eventually re-hitting $100 million in annual EBITDA, as I forecasted in my article published in March, perhaps improvements in the coming fiscal year will be much more modest.

Admittedly, even if annual EBITDA were to only improve TTM levels to, say, $75 million, this would likely have a materially positive impact on the PLCE stock price. Assuming PLCE were to sustain its current valuation, $75 million in annual EBITDA would translate into an enterprise value of around $1.04 billion.

Net of debt and lease liabilities, and adding cash position, we get a net valuation of around $483.9 million, or around $22 per share, more than double the current share price.

Even so, this is before taking into account the potential for additional shareholder dilution. Still operating in the red operationally, and with relatively little cash on hand, further rounds of equity financing/debt-for-equity swaps appear likely.

Dilution Factor Worsens Risk/Reward Proposition

As I've put it before, Mithaq looks to be in the middle of a "takeunder" of the company. That is, instead of this fund taking the company private, buying out minority shareholders at a premium, Mithaq has instead capitalized on PLCE's falling stock price, to convert debt to equity at lower prices, increasing the fund's position in the company along the way.

Interestingly enough, this reminds me of another situation involving an unprofitable company highly dependent on steady outside capital. EV maker Lucid Group (LCID). LCID is down by nearly 80% since its early-2021 heyday at the height of the EV bubble.

Shifting sentiment on EV stocks, not to mention high losses for this early-stage company, may be largely to blame. However, it should be noted that the Public Investment Fund (PIF), the sovereign wealth fund of Saudi Arabia, has bought newly-issued shares of LCID, resulting in major shareholder dilution, as Seeking Alpha contributor Anna Sokolidou in her Aug. 2025 article on Lucid stock.

I wouldn't rule out the potential of further dilution with The Children's Place. As reiterated in the earnings press release, alongside with $43.8 million in additional borrowing availability under its revolving credit facility, the company could also tap into an additional $40 million in unsecured funding offered by Mithaq.

If PLCE were to have to tap into this additional financing source, presumably converting the loan into equity as it's done in the past, this would lead to a new round of shareholder dilution, albeit not as dramatic as the $90 million rights offering that occurred at the start of this year.

Nevertheless, even if the impact of dilution could be moderate at worst, this would decrease the potential upside, all while downside risk remains substantial. Prior rounds of aggressive cost cutting have yet to yield consistently-better results for the company.

It remains speculative to say whether the latest round of improvements, this time focused more on increased investment in the brand, the company's stores, and other efforts to increase store traffic. Consumer sentiment has continued to worsen, calling further into question how quickly, and to what extent, this company's operating performance will start improving in a big way.

A New Silver Lining, Final Thoughts, and the Bottom Line

Although my view on PLCE has become decidedly less bullish, I should note a possible newly-emerged silver lining with this name: the recent purchase of this stock by Immersion (IMMR), a name that I have covered several times here on Seeking Alpha, including recently.

Immersion's main business is in patent licensing, the company owns a large portfolio of haptic technology patents that it monetizes through litigation, but under the leadership of investors Eric Singer and William Martin, has become a holding company, investing excess capital into numerous positions in publicly-traded companies.

Many of these companies are also in the patent licensing business/hold patent portfolio, but IMMR's largest holding right now is its minority stake in Barnes & Noble Education (BNED), and as noted in Immersion's latest 13F filing, the company now owns around 2.6% of PLCE's outstanding shares.

While I wouldn't buy PLCE just because these two investors are also seemingly bullish on an eventual Children's Place turnaround, perhaps I am not looking at the big picture, of this company not only getting back to levels of profitability from the recent past, but back to pre-Covid levels of profitability.

Who knows? Next week, The Children's Place is presenting at the Sidoti Small Cap Conference. Further information about the turnaround could be revealed in PLCE's presentation. Once the investor presentation shown at this conference becomes public, it may provide further food for thought with regards to the turnaround bull case.

Nevertheless, until then, or until subsequent quarterly results demonstrate that PLCE's results are improving, as opposed to getting "less bad," it may be best to hold off on this name, especially as it experiences price volatility due to short-squeeze speculation.

data.title: Legrand: A Very International Industrial Group With Data Centers
data.description:
Increasing investments focused on either companies with international operations or companies that are located in areas that are growing faster than the Western world is something I have been pushing for for over 2 years. Every week, I try to find one or two companies that I haven't heard of before to start researching their operations and see if they could be a suitable investment for me at some point.

Legrand SA (OTCPK:LGRDY) is not a company I have been unaware of, but a company I hadn't researched until a month or so ago. In this article, the first on Legrand for me, I will establish a valuation target/price target and show you why I consider the company to be interesting. French companies, in general, have, in my experience, a tendency to be more internationally focused, but also focused internationally in a way that's not just "EU, Asia, and NA". They tend to often focus on areas like the Middle East, Africa, and South America, in part due to tradition but also due to history.

This specific company is an "industrial group", which doesn't tell us much, but we'll get into exactly what it is and what the company does.

Legrand—Conservative French Industrial with an international focus

So what does it mean when a company has an "international focus"? It means that, in this case, the company gets more than 80% of its revenues from international sales.

It also means that the company is established in over 85 nations, with product distribution covering more than twice the amount. This leaves Legrand products as being sold in more countries in the world than they are "not" sold in. It has a 150+ year history at this point and was originally a manufacturer of Porcelain out of beautiful Limoges, in France. This porcelain factory then turned, in the early 20th century, into an electric switch company (from porcelain and boxwood). This came to be the company's step-by-step focus as it moved more and more into the field of electrical equipment and engineering.

Remember, this was before plastic was available, so porcelain is not actually an odd product—it was the best and one of the most malleable insulating materials of the time. Even today, many older breaker boxes in Sweden have components made from porcelain.

Legrand has been an inorganically focused grower. It has done over 100 of them, all in the field of building infrastructure and electrics, and now sells over 300k items. It went outside France in 1966, into South America in -77, the US in -84, and has only grown since that time.

Legrand fundamentals are very solid. We're talking A-rating, we're talking a market cap of about €38B, and we're talking, unfortunately, a low yield of sub 1.7% even at today's share price. This means that when investing in Legrand, you need to make sure that capital appreciation from earnings growth or increased premiumization mostly covers your returns - because yield will be a small contributor to it.

Still, the company mostly hits its targets historically—it has impressive accuracy numbers.

Looking at the last annual sales numbers, most of the company's products go to the EU and NA, but there's a growing Africa/Middle East and South American operation, and without the datacenter segment, the company would have seen much slower amounts of organic growth.

Legrand is inherently profitable, very volatile, and generates consistent cash flow, with a net profit margin of over 13% and an FCF conversion of 15% of sales or close to it. Given revenues for 2024, that meant FCF of close to €1.3B. The debt ratio is less than 1.6x of EBITDA, and Legrand has over €2B in cash available.

Legrand plays a critical role in the development of data center infrastructure because it provides the electrical and digital backbone for the industrial locations where this is made. The company does everything from server rooms to power solutions for centers to entire cooling systems.

It's entirely accurate, therefore, to say that by investing in Legrand, you're investing in Data centers, and there are many concrete examples around the world, from the EU, SA, and NA to Asia, where the company has done this.

Because the company isn't exposed to the risks of data center operation or management, only construction, cooling, containment, and things like cable management, I view the risk factor here as favorable when compared to investing in a general data center operator. The company's data center solutions have already been implemented in over 90% of its countries where products are available.

This goes in line with my overall investment strategy. I like owning manufacturers of technology and their support rather than owning much of the technology itself. For instance, I'd rather own this company supporting the construction and planning of data centers than own companies that manage data centers and deal with clients. I'd rather own manufacturers of food processing equipment than plants that produce food (though in that example, both are potentially attractive, but with the former clearly more so).

And the company has been accelerating its push into data centers as well as other sectors (which also, of course, influence data centers).

These acquisitions will bring another quarter-billion to sales, with a 100% sales amount increase in Oceania and a heavier tilt towards digital transition and energy. Less has been invested in what the company calls "essential electrical infrastructure", but that's because the company already has quite a leadership here.

It's not unfair to characterize Legrand as a push into energy transition, smart control for homes and buildings, and physical data center solutions like cable management and the like. The last results for the company were solid—half-year results, with a sales growth of 15% and a continued 20%+ operating margin. Data centers drove growth, mostly inorganic, and the company's quarterly results saw the company increase its forecasts for the full year to up to 12%.

Surprisingly, there is a massive increase in growth in North & Central America, 20% for the YoY period. This has to do with datacenter offerings in the US, which saw the company grow significantly, and highlights the importance of future growth.

This, of course, begs the question: given the importance of the US and datacenters, what about tariffs? Key exposure for Legrand is about 50% of COGS, which is, of course, significant. So what can Legrand do to offset what is expected, if things are upheld, to be a 15% increase?

The company is targeting sales price increases, efficiencies, supply chain adjustments, several adoptions to lower the percentage of COGS exposed to tariffs, and other things. But this key risk is, as I see it, still not 100% sure. The tariffs are currently subject to reversal by the U.S. Supreme Court. It's a risk overhang, but not a 100% certain one. And even if tariffs were to come, I believe the company would find a way to cope, even if I expect a full 1-2% impact on operating margin, with a €30M impact expected from the tariffs on Chinese goods alone.

Going into the remainder of 2025, the balance sheet is solid—1.5x debt, and what debt there is funded at a 90% fixed rate with over 6 years' maturity. There's not much volatility beyond tariffs and macro that I can see, and maybe ups and downs from sectors like data centers.

Future growth areas for the company are expected to come from things like access control, healthcare, data centers, power busbars, and general so-called white space infrastructure (mostly data centers/IT).

So let's look at what valuation dictates about the company here.

Valuation for Legrand—upside exists, but I would say not at this valuation.

There are macro and smaller risks that need to be considered when you put a price and fair value target on a company like Legrand. Macro risks would be things like the higher interest rates we've seen since ZIRP, which have effectively reduced residential and nonresidential building spending. This, in turn, lowers demand for the company's products, so an exposure to building and construction is clear, aside from data centers. Furthermore, Legrand has a significant CRE/office building exposure, and it's no secret in real estate that this sector has underperformed as well. Aside from these exposures and data centers, the largest risk for the company that curtails the fair value and share price is the 40%+ COGS to US exposure—or tariff exposure, to put it bluntly.

This needs to be put next to multiples. Legrand, over the past 20 years, has traded close to about 19x P/E—it's currently almost 7x above that, at close to 26x P/E. This would represent an overvaluation in most companies, and it does for Legrand as well. Even in the likely event (based on historical accuracy) of the company hitting its 8-9% EPS growth targets, the company is expensive here.

At the 20-year average forecast, the company forecasts results in a negative 4% growth for the company until the 2027 fiscal year.

So it will require the company to outperform quite a bit over time in terms of either above-8-9% EPS growth, as forecasted, or allowing for a 20-26x P/E. I view that as being too much.

I forecast 8% EPS growth annualized, of which 3% is expected to be inorganic from continued M&As. The company's 4-5% expected organic growth rate is below the peer average for construction and infrastructure companies, but due to the high exposure to CRE and Residential as opposed to infrastructure, I believe in a lower growth rate here. The growth will instead come from continued data center expansion (though the rate of growth here is also uncertain, and tariffs make it even more so). I don't see a massively declining or a massively growing margin; instead, I see the ups and downsides taking one another out, to a 20-21% OM—but I do see this as a company with potential volatility.

So, earnings forecasts do not imply a good return to us here. Using the highest 5-year average 22x P/E, the annualized RoR here is less than 3% per year, even with the company's 1.6% yield.

So where does this leave us? I believe we need the company cheaper than this. I'm willing, based on 8-9% growth, to give the company a 19-21x P/E range. That gives the company a fair value target of €125/share for the 2027E P/E. The Fair value target is how high I believe the company can go—not where I'd want to buy. To get a 15% annualized Upside to this, we'd need to buy the company at a double-digit share price. That's why I put my price target, the target you'd want to buy at, at €100/share.

In doing so, I'm putting my target significantly below the current analyst average. The average is currently at €130/share, but I'd ask you to look at how quickly this target has moved up.

Beyond this, I'd look at peers. Peers for Legrand are companies such as Schneider (OTCPK:SBGSY) and ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) in the same fields. Both of these are also overvalued, however, which means that the sector is currently considered to be too expensive for investment. I own neither company at this time.

The conclusion here is that the company is an attractive one, but not at this valuation. I'd wait until it's about €100/share or below.

Risks to my thesis are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risks to my neutral thesis on the company are Legrand's positioning for solid investments into efficiency and IoT/connected infrastructure. If demand grows here, the company can obviously go significantly higher, even though it would require the company to get past tariffs and data center volatility. Data center demand could also rise, which could support the company.

Furthermore, Legrand, while being a "standard" electrical component company, also has a strong reputation for excellent products and reliability. In fact, among the CAC 40, it scores the second best for reputation, even though this was some time ago.

Furthermore, the company's balance sheet is stellar, which also needs to be considered. Further upside is possible—I just believe it to be unlikely.

Thesis

  • Legrand is one of the premier electrical component companies out there. It's A-rated, has managed superb growth, and mostly hits its targets and forecasts. Even the latest set of results is of a positive nature and doesn't give us much reason to doubt for the future.
  • The problem is most definitely the company's valuation, which is over 20% above its 20-year average and 10% above its 5-year average. I view the company as overvalued because of this and would say it's a "Hold".
  • I give the company a price target of below or at €100/share to get a 15% annualized conservative upside here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of €100/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of September 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Calamos Global Opportunities Strategy Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Summary

  • The Calamos Global Opportunities Strategy leverages Calamos’ capital structure research to invest in equities and equity-sensitive securities of global growth companies, in order to generate consistent alpha and manage downside volatility versus the MSCI All Country World Index over a full market cycle.
  • During the first half of 2025, international markets’ strong performance demonstrated that America First policies would not be an impediment, as many feared.
  • Rather, while the US stock market continued to provide compelling opportunities, the market increasingly recognized opportunities in international markets.
  • We expect the tailwinds for international markets to strengthen, supported by fiscal and monetary policies, country- and region-specific themes, and individual company fundamentals.

Investment Manager Discussion

During the second quarter, international equities powered ahead, extending a year-to-date lead over the US equity market, while the dollar continued to weaken. International small caps performed with particular strength.

As the second half of 2025 begins, we believe the case for global and international allocations is only growing stronger. Below, we highlight key considerations from our posts and papers of recent months.

US Exceptionalism Fades, International Opportunities Rise to the Forefront

For many years, a dominant narrative of US exceptionalism led many investors to look to the United States as the “only game in town.” This preference for US equities reflected a degree of tunnel vision, but of course, it was not unfounded. Many of the most innovative companies in the world are domiciled in the United States, and a backdrop of globalism has provided US companies with many opportunities to optimize supply chains, and in turn, maximize profits. Further, foreign holdings of US assets increased in tandem with persistent and rising trade deficits with the rest of the world, providing an additional boost to US investment performance. A weakening dollar further highlighted that a significant shift is underway. (Our post, “Why the Dollar Inflection Should Not be Ignored” and a recent Chart of the Week "This time was different” explores some trends in the dollar with interesting asset allocation implications.)

To be clear, we believe that many US equities will continue to offer compelling growth. Despite a chaotic start to the year on the policy front, the US economy seems positioned for continued growth, supported by a resilient consumer and healthy business spending. Policy clarity is coming into focus, which can provide additional tailwinds for US companies.

Yet, we also see an international revival. This resurgence of international opportunity has largely resulted from the Trump administration’s America First policies, which have compelled many economies to pursue their own economic growth policies more aggressively. In March, we wrote in our post “Overseas Investment Opportunities: US Policy Shifts Awaken the Sleeping Giants,” that America First policies “don’t necessarily mean US equities will lead—and they certainly don’t preclude investment opportunities overseas.”

We noted that policy shifts would catalyze other global economies to adapt, innovate, and pursue stimulative economic policies. These policies, in turn, could drive sustained relative outperformance in overseas equity markets.

This trend has indeed proven to be the case, and we are increasingly excited about the tailwinds for growth that we see worldwide. As the US pursues its more domestic-centric agenda, we are also seeing regional- and country-specific growth drivers gather force around the world.

Germany. So far this year, Germany has been the textbook example for stimulating regional growth. The country spent much of the post-Global Financial Crisis (GFC) era restraining spending and investment, but is now relaxing constitutional debt limits and embarking on a significant fiscal stimulus of more than €600 billion over the next five years. Other countries across the Eurozone will be following suit. We believe companies in the defense, industrials, materials, and financial services industries are among those best positioned to benefit from the improved spending and growth environment.

Emerging markets. We are also finding many opportunities supported by local trends in emerging markets, including China, India and Korea. As we discussed in our “Sleeping Giants” post, Beijing’s complacency regarding letting the US innovate while China made and sold goods to the US is rapidly changing. After striking a much more antagonistic tone toward Chinese tech leaders, Chinese policymakers have shifted the discourse to show support toward home-grown innovation and development. More broadly, China is focusing greater attention on diversifying its economy and strengthening its own domestic market. We are finding many opportunities to capitalize on the inflection points coming out of China.

India also remains one of the most exciting growth stories in the world, one that we’ve long recognized across the consumer and enterprise.

Opportunities across the capitalization spectrum. As the world becomes “less global,” smaller international companies are poised to offer targeted entry points into regional- and country-specific growth trends. Not only are non-US small caps poised to generate outsized growth potential as international markets gather steam, but they are also attractively valued and often more exposed to local growth drivers.

The case for global convertibles. The Calamos Global Team has a deep history in comprehensive capital structure research, including decades of experience in convertible securities. Because convertibles blend characteristics of stocks and bonds, they can provide upside equity market participation while mitigating downside risk. Leveraging this favorable risk-reward skew, we continue to use convertible structures in certain portfolios to provide lower-volatility participation in certain high-growth (but potentially more volatile) names and individual markets where we seek to actively manage a more asymmetric risk profile.

Nick Niziolek, CFA

Co-CIO and Senior Co-Portfolio Manager

Dennis Cogan, CFA

SVP and Senior Co-Portfolio Manager

Paul Ryndak, CFA

SVP and Associate Portfolio Manager

Kyle Ruge, CFA

VP and Associate Portfolio Manager

July 1, 2025

Market Environment

Global equities rallied in the second quarter, as investors responded to progress on global trade negotiations, resilient economic growth, and a weaker dollar. The broad-based MSCI ACWI Index returned 11.69% during the quarter. Developed markets returned 11.63%, as measured by the MSCI World Index, and US equities returned 10.94%, as reflected by the S&P 500 Index. Emerging markets also rallied and outperformed in the quarter, based on the MSCI Emerging Markets Index return of 12.20%.

Sectors within the MSCI ACWI Index delivered mostly positive returns in the quarter. Technology (+23.38%, USD terms) and communication services (+17.99%) led the index, while health care (-3.53%) and energy (-3.50%) trailed other sectors for the period.

Following the early April selloff, US equity shares surged higher in the quarter as investors responded to headway on global trade discussions, positive earnings revisions, and renewed rate cut hopes. Corporate earnings remain healthy, with over 75% of S&P 500 companies beating estimates and a positive trend in earnings revisions as the quarter progressed. Regarding the broad economy, recent data pointed to a bounce back in GDP growth in the second quarter and cooling inflation trends despite persistent tariff concerns. With respect to monetary policy, the Fed left interest rates unchanged at multiple meetings, including the latest pause in June, which marked the fourth consecutive meeting with no change.

European equities returned 11.80% in the quarter, as reflected in the MSCI Europe Index (USD terms, 3.37% in local currency). Individual countries saw positive returns, reflecting an upbeat policy outlook, improved business confidence, and rising capital inflows. Based on recent macro data, the European economy continues to see modest growth and easing inflation. The headline rate hovered near the ECB target at approximately 2% recently, as slowing services inflation made a notable impact. From a monetary perspective, the ECB cut interest rates multiple times in the quarter to support growth amid lower inflation and trade uncertainty. In terms of country performance, Portugal and Austria led other markets (in USD terms), returning 22.47% and 21.00%, while Denmark and Switzerland trailed other regions in the quarter, returning 7.50% and 8.11%, respectively.

Developed Asia Pacific also saw robust returns during the second quarter (in USD terms), as the MSCI Pacific Index returned 12.33% (in USD, 8.37% in local currency). Japanese equities generated strong gains in the quarter. From a macro perspective, business and consumer confidence have improved recently, with strength in services activity supported by a pickup in new orders and resilient global demand. On the monetary policy front, the Bank of Japan held its benchmark rate steady at 0.5% over numerous meetings, underscoring its cautious stance amid geopolitical risks and tariff meetings. Australian shares also rallied in the quarter, despite the region’s mixed economic data and ongoing concerns about the potential impact of tariffs on trade volume. Recent data showed a notable decline in exports to the US, while sales to China have been a source of strength. Hong Kong equities rose sharply in the quarter, adding to the region’s strong year-to-date returns. In terms of select country performance, Japan returned 11.39%, while Australia and Hong Kong returned 15.10% and 15.80%, respectively (in USD terms).

Emerging markets outperformed developed markets in the second quarter, as the MSCI Emerging Markets Index returned 12.20% (in USD terms, 8.13% in local currency). Chinese equities rose modestly in the quarter, adding to strong double-digit returns year-to-date. Shares reflect mixed dynamics, benefiting from government stimulus initiatives and strength in the service sector, but also confronting persistent uncertainty on trade negotiations with the US. Stocks in India saw strong returns of over 9% in the second quarter. India’s economy continued to grow, with PMI data showing expansion in services and manufacturing activity while benefiting from lower consumer prices. Korean equities rallied during the quarter on the heels of elections and an improved demand environment. Among individual EM countries, the leaders in the period included Korea (+32.80%) and Taiwan (+26.29%) in USD terms, while the laggards included Saudi Arabia (-5.03%) and Thailand (+0.54%).

Global Convertible Market. Global convertibles saw positive returns across regions in the quarter, reflecting the performance of the underlying equities and demand for the asset class. Global convertibles returned 8.45%, as measured by the ICE BofA Global 300 Index, (in USD terms). US convertibles returned 8.88% in the quarter, as represented by the ICE BofA All US Convertibles Index. European convertibles returned 14.63% in the quarter and Japan euro convertibles returned 6.57% (both in USD terms). Asia ex-Japan convertibles returned 5.98% in the period (in USD terms). Global convertible issuance totaled a robust $47.1 billion in the second quarter per BofA data. From a regional perspective, the US led issuance with $32.5 billion, Europe raised $4.8 billion, Asia ex-Japan introduced $9.8 billion, while Japan saw no new issuance.

Performance Review

Global equities rallied in the second quarter, as investors responded to signs of progress on global trade negotiations, resilient economic growth, and a weaker dollar. The Calamos Global Opportunities Strategy returned 14.83% (gross of fees) and 14.63% (net of fees) versus the 11.69% return of the MSCI ACWI index. The portfolio’s sector allocation and relative security selection added value. Many of the portfolio’s holdings that aligned with our key secular themes and possessed higher growth fundamentals performed well in the period.

Positive Influences on Performance

Information Technology. The portfolio benefited from security selection and an average overweight position in information technology, where holdings in the technology hardware, storage & peripherals and internet services & infrastructure industries added to relative returns.

Health Care. An average underweight stance in health care buoyed the portfolio’s performance. Specifically, pharmaceuticals and our lack of exposure to managed health care promoted relative returns.

Negative Influences on Performance

Utilities. Over the period, security selection and an average overweight position within the utilities sector were detrimental to performance. Holdings in electric utilities and our lack of representation in independent power producers & energy traders held back relative returns.

Consumer Discretionary. Security selection within the consumer discretionary sector’s broadline retail and hotels, resorts & cruise lines industries lagged.

Geographic Performance

Favorable security selection and an average overweight allocation in the United States added to portfolio performance. Leading security selection in Europe promoted relative results. Investments in Germany and the United Kingdom notably added significant value.

Conversely, security selection in Emerging Asia had a negative impact on the portfolio’s performance, specifically in China and South Korea. Securities in Canada also set back performance.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

From a sector perspective, information technology, industrials, and communication services are the largest absolute weights in the portfolio.

Key industry positions include semiconductors, systems software, diversified banks, aerospace & defense, movies & entertainment, broadline retail, and electric utilities.

We are underweight in health care, real estate, and financials overall.

In the US, we own a diversified position across industries. We see bottom-up opportunities across our key secular themes, reflecting our emphasis on companies with advantaged business models and strong fundamentals.

In Europe, we hold a blend of secular growth, cyclicals, and higher-quality defensives. Given the region’s extensive linkages to global trade, we continue to evaluate the impact of fiscal stimulus and monetary easing while monitoring evolving trade dynamics.

We own a range of holdings in emerging markets. Our positioning reflects our view of key policy reforms and an increase in localized consumption. We also evaluate news on tariffs and their potential impacts on EM economies.

We hold multiple opportunities in Japan and favor balanced exposures across secular and cyclical growth opportunities.

For additional information, please visit the strategy’s profile page:

https://www.calamos.com/strategies/global-opportunities/

Past performance does not guarantee or indicate future results. Portfolios are managed according to their respective strategies which may differ significantly in terms of security holdings, industry weightings and asset allocation from those of the benchmark(s). Portfolio performance, characteristics and volatility may differ from the benchmark(s) shown.

For Institutional Use Only

The information portrayed is for Calamos Global Opportunities Strategy and as such only relate to the representative portfolio shown. Representative holdings and portfolio characteristics are specific only to the portfolio shown at that point in time. Other portfolios will vary in composition, characteristics, and will experience different investment results.
